message.content:
You are an INDEPENDENT verifier adjudicating audit finding GitHub issue #11 for /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git mutation, no gh. Use grep/Read and /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.

FINDING (#11, stated severity MEDIUM): src/assay_engine/orchestration/gates.py module docstring (lines 1-11) promises "the engine records the decision to the append-only provenance trail before the next phase runs" and a requires_human human-in-the-loop flag, but Gate.evaluate() (lines 33-57) records nothing and never reads requires_human; no provenance/audit module exists in the package, though GOVERNANCE.md and METHODOLOGY.md §7 promise an append-only audit trail.

Independently verify: read gates.py; grep -rni "provenance|audit|append" src/; confirm requires_human is never read and no provenance module exists. Consider the counter-argument that the docstring hedges with "when wired" (is the drift overstated?). Decide TRUE/PARTIAL/FALSE and whether the right fix is docstring-softening vs a fail-loud provenance stub. Assess MEDIUM severity.

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the gates.py file and searching for provenance/audit infrastructure.

content.content:
1	"""Governance gates — deterministic, code-enforced transition checks.
2	
3	A gate guards a transition between phases. It evaluates code-enforced preconditions (the
4	firewalls, lock state, provenance recorded) and, where the governance model requires it,
5	records a human approval. A gate cannot be silently bypassed: the transition is only legal
6	if the gate returns an ``approved`` decision, and the engine records the decision to the
7	append-only provenance trail before the next phase runs.
8	
9	The concrete gate set and the human-in-the-loop wiring are lifted from the prior platform's
10	governance graph; this module fixes the gate contract.
11	"""
12	
13	from __future__ import annotations
14	
15	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
16	from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping
17	
18	from assay_engine.orchestration.phases import Phase
19	
20	
21	class GateError(RuntimeError):
22	    """Raised when a transition is attempted through a gate that did not approve it."""
23	
24	
25	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
26	class GateDecision:
27	    approved: bool
28	    gate: str
29	    reason: str
30	    evidence: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
31	
32	
33	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
34	class Gate:
35	    """A guard on the transition ``frm -> to``.
36	
37	    ``precondition`` is a pure, code-enforced check over the run context returning a
38	    :class:`GateDecision`. ``requires_human`` marks gates that additionally need a recorded
39	    operator approval (handled by the orchestration layer when wired).
40	    """
41	
42	    name: str
43	    frm: Phase
44	    to: Phase
45	    precondition: Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], GateDecision]
46	    requires_human: bool = False
47	
48	    def evaluate(self, context: Mapping[str, Any]) -> GateDecision:
49	        if not self.frm.can_advance_to(self.to):
50	            raise GateError(
51	                f"gate {self.name!r} declares an illegal transition "
52	                f"{self.frm.name} -> {self.to.name}"
53	            )
54	        decision = self.precondition(context)
55	        if not decision.approved:
56	            raise GateError(f"gate {self.name!r} blocked transition: {decision.reason}")
57	        return decision
58

---section 7---
## 7. Reproducibility

- **Determinism:** fixed, documented seeds; inputs hashed; model names + versions recorded.
- **Provenance:** an append-only audit trail records every action before the next executes.
- **Pre-registration:** hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and
  RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation. This is honestly described as an adaptive
  design with a timestamped lock — not as predictions guessed before any data.
- **Reproducibility package:** data, code, configuration, and logs are published with
  findings so any reviewer can reconstruct the full decision history.

## 8. Scoped-independence caveat

content.text: Now let me check the GOVERNANCE.md section 3 and the toolkit.py reference to confirm whether provenance is described as a future/aspirational item or a live feature.

content.content:
## 3. Provenance

An **append-only** audit trail (W3C PROV-DM style) records every action before the next
executes. No step can be retroactively removed or reordered. This is the spine of
reproducibility and of the independence claim.

## 4. Single-operator structural independence

1	"""Baseline builder contract + a deterministic artifact wrapper.
2	
3	The builders (embeddings, similarity, graph, clustering, stats) are implemented as the
4	engine matures; this module fixes the *shape* of a baseline artifact and the build contract
5	so the rest of the engine (gates, confirm, provenance) can be wired against it now.
6	
7	Firewall A is enforced here structurally: :meth:`BaselineBuilder.build` accepts a
8	:class:`~assay_engine.methodology.firewalls.ClaimBlindGuard` and never accepts a claims
9	source — a baseline simply cannot be handed the claims it must stay blind to.
10	"""
11	
12	from __future__ import annotations
13	
14	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
15	from typing import Any, Mapping, Protocol, runtime_checkable
16	
17	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
18	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard
19	
20

content.text:
I now have a complete picture. Let me confirm there's no provenance module anywhere and assess the docstring's hedging carefully.

The evidence is clear. Let me finalize my analysis.

**Key facts established:**

1. **gates.py docstring (lines 1-11)** states: "the engine records the decision to the append-only provenance trail before the next phase runs" and describes `requires_human` marking gates that "additionally need a recorded operator approval."

2. **`Gate.evaluate()` (lines 48-57)** does only: illegal-transition check → call `precondition` → raise on not-approved → return decision. It records **nothing** and never reads `self.requires_human`.

3. **No provenance/audit module exists**: the full `find src -name "*.py"` listing shows no provenance.py, audit.py, or trail module. The only matches for "provenance/audit/append" in src are (a) the `Claim.provenance` data field in contracts/claims.py (an unrelated metadata dict), (b) docstring *mentions* in toolkit.py and gates.py, and (c) docstring comments — none are an executable append-only trail.

4. **`requires_human` is never read** anywhere in src (only defined at gates.py:46 and described in docstring at :38). Confirmed by grep.

5. **Docs do promise it as live infrastructure**: GOVERNANCE.md §3 ("An append-only audit trail … records every action before the next executes. No step can be retroactively removed or reordered") and METHODOLOGY.md §7 ("an append-only audit trail records every action before the next executes"). GOVERNANCE.md §1 lists "provenance store live" as a gate precondition.

**Counter-argument assessment (the hedging):**
- The `Gate` class docstring (line 39) does hedge `requires_human`: "handled by the orchestration layer when wired." That hedge is accurate and fair for the flag itself.
- BUT the *module* docstring (lines 5-7) is not hedged: it asserts as present-tense fact that "the engine records the decision to the append-only provenance trail before the next phase runs" and "A gate cannot be silently bypassed." Since no provenance trail exists and `evaluate()` records nothing, a gate decision is in fact NOT recorded — the strongest safety claim is false. The hedge on one sentence doesn't cure the unhedged claim on another.

VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE
CONFIDENCE: high
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: MEDIUM is appropriate, arguably at the upper edge of MEDIUM. This is a pre-scaffold engine where most builders are explicitly "implemented as the engine matures," so no runtime safety regression exists today (nothing calls `evaluate()` in a real pipeline, and `requires_human` defaults False). The harm is documentation/contract drift: GOVERNANCE.md §3 elevates the append-only trail to "the spine of … the independence claim," and §1 lists "provenance store live" as a gate precondition, yet the code that claims to enforce it records nothing. For a governance/independence-centric project this is a credibility/trust defect, not merely cosmetic — hence MEDIUM, not LOW. Not HIGH because there is no live bypass of a real running gate.

REASONING: Independently confirmed all three claims. (1) `Gate.evaluate()` body contains only a transition-legality check, a precondition call, and a not-approved raise; it neither writes any record nor references `self.requires_human`. (2) `grep -rni "requires_human" src/` returns only the field definition (gates.py:46) and its own docstring (gates.py:38) — never a read. (3) `find src -name "*.py"` shows no provenance/audit module; the only "provenance" code is the unrelated `Claim.provenance` metadata field. Meanwhile docs/GOVERNANCE.md §3 and docs/METHODOLOGY.md §7 promise a live, present-tense append-only audit trail "before the next executes." The module docstring's unhedged sentence ("the engine records the decision to the append-only provenance trail before the next phase runs" / "A gate cannot be silently bypassed") is therefore factually false against the current code. The drift is real, not overstated — though the finding slightly overreaches by implying `requires_human`'s description is undocumented-as-future; line 39 does hedge that specific flag with "when wired," so credit is due there. That partial hedge is why the issue is real but bounded.

FIX_GUIDANCE: Two-part. (a) Immediate, low-cost: soften the module docstring (lines 5-7) to future/aspirational tense matching the class-level hedge — e.g. "When the orchestration layer is wired, the engine will record the decision to the append-only provenance trail; today `evaluate()` enforces only the transition and precondition checks." This removes the false present-tense safety guarantee. (b) Stronger, recommended given governance centrality: rather than only soften prose, make the gap fail-loud — add a provenance sink hook to `evaluate()` (or a thin `provenance.py` stub) such that a gate with `requires_human=True`, or any evaluate call lacking a wired recorder, raises `GateError`/`NotImplementedError` instead of silently returning approved. That way the "cannot be silently bypassed" claim becomes true-by-construction (no recorder ⇒ no transition), preventing the contract from being quietly violated as builders are filled in. Docstring-softening alone is acceptable for MEDIUM, but the fail-loud stub better matches GOVERNANCE.md's framing of provenance as the spine of the independence claim.
